_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 10-1 General CBRNE Identification (MDHHS Reviewed 2023-05-08). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Special Operations Protocol Section 10-1: General CBRNE Identification — MDHHS Reviewed 2023
purpose: Provide EMS providers with recognition criteria, safety guidance, and initial medical response for Chemical, Biological, Radiological, Nuclear, and Explosive (CBRNE) events.

sections.title: CBRNE Agent Categories
content: Chemical (C): Nerve agents, vesicants, choking agents, blood agents, lacrimators — typically rapid onset, multiple simultaneous casualties with similar presentations
Biological (B): Bacteria, viruses, toxins — often delayed presentation (hours to days); may present as unusual disease cluster
Radiological (R): Radioactive material dispersal (RDD/'dirty bomb') — no immediate symptoms; long-term exposure risk; detect with dosimeter
Nuclear (N): Nuclear detonation — blast, thermal, radiation effects; mass destruction; near-total EMS system involvement
Explosive (E): Conventional or improvised explosive devices — blast overpressure, fragmentation, thermal burns; secondary device risk

sections.title: Scene Recognition — Indicators of CBRNE Event
content: Multiple patients with similar unexplained symptoms in a common area or timeframe
Unusual odors (bitter almonds = cyanide; garlic/mustard = vesicants; freshly cut grass/new-mown hay = phosgene)
Unusual vapor clouds, mists, oily films, or residues
Dead or dying animals in area
Suspicious devices, containers, or packages
Reported explosion with or without fire
Patients with unexplained neurological symptoms (seizures, miosis, excessive secretions — nerve agent)
Patients with unexplained burns, blistering, skin/eye irritation (vesicant/lacrimator)
Unexplained mass casualty event

sections.title: Scene Safety — Initial Approach
content: Do NOT enter scene without appropriate PPE — identify hazard level before approach
Isolate and deny entry — establish hot/warm/cold zones with fire/HazMat
Notify incident command, HazMat, and online medical control immediately
Approach from upwind and uphill when possible
Limit number of EMS personnel in hot zone — only trained HazMat/EMS personnel with appropriate PPE
Self-protection is the first priority — a contaminated rescuer becomes a second victim

sections.title: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) Levels
content: Level A: Highest protection — fully encapsulated chemical-resistant suit with SCBA; used when agent is unknown or highest vapor/liquid risk; NOT typically worn by EMS
Level B: SCBA with non-encapsulating chemical-resistant suit; liquid splash protection; used when agent identified but vapor threat low
Level C: Air-purifying respirator (APR/PAPR) with chemical-resistant clothing; used when agent type and concentration known and APR is adequate
Level D: Standard work uniform with minimal protection; used when no respiratory or skin hazard present (clean/cold zone only)

sections.title: Chemical Agent Sub-Types
content: Nerve Agents (persistent and non-persistent): GA (Tabun), GB (Sarin), GD (Soman), VX — cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGEM); miosis; refer to Protocol 10-3
Vesicants/Blister Agents: Mustard (HD), Lewisite (L), Phosgene Oxime (CX) — delayed blistering, airway burns; decontaminate immediately
Choking/Pulmonary Agents: Chlorine, Phosgene, Diphosgene — airway/pulmonary irritation; pulmonary edema; refer to Protocol 10-2
Blood Agents/Cyanide: Hydrogen cyanide (HCN), Cyanogen chloride — cellular hypoxia; refer to Protocol 10-5
Lacrimators/Riot Control Agents: CS, CN, OC (pepper spray) — eye/airway irritation; rarely life-threatening; refer to Protocol 10-2
Persistent agents: remain hazardous in environment for hours to days (VX, mustard); non-persistent: dissipate rapidly (sarin, cyanide)

sections.title: Biological Agent Indicators
content: Sudden appearance of large numbers of ill patients with similar febrile illness
Unusual disease or presentation not normally seen in region
Higher morbidity/mortality than expected for apparent illness
Multiple simultaneous outbreaks in geographically distinct areas
Illness concentrated in area consistent with plume or aerosol exposure
Notify public health authorities and medical control early — biological events are primarily a public health response

sections.title: Radiological/Nuclear Indicators
content: Reported explosion in area with known or suspected radiological material
Unusual number of patients with nausea, vomiting, hair loss, skin burns without obvious thermal source
Detection via radiation dosimeter or Geiger counter (fire/HazMat responsibility)
EMS role: limit exposure (time, distance, shielding), treat conventional injuries, decontaminate externally contaminated patients before transport
Acute radiation syndrome (ARS): nausea/vomiting, fatigue, skin erythema — dose-dependent; no specific prehospital antidote

sections.title: Explosive Incident Considerations
content: Primary blast injury: overpressure wave — hollow organ (tympanic membrane, lung, bowel) most vulnerable
Secondary blast injury: fragmentation wounds
Tertiary blast injury: displacement of body by blast wind
Quaternary blast injury: burns, crush, toxin inhalation
SECONDARY DEVICE AWARENESS: assume secondary device risk at any explosive incident; maintain safe perimeter until cleared by EOD
Combined conventional/CBRNE events (dirty bombs): treat conventional injuries first after decontamination

sections.title: General EMS Response — All CBRNE Events
content: 1. Recognize — identify signs of CBRNE event; do NOT approach without PPE
2. Notify — alert dispatch, IC, HazMat, online medical control, hospital
3. Isolate — establish hot/warm/cold zones; restrict access
4. Protect — don appropriate PPE before entry
5. Decontaminate — gross decon by fire/HazMat before EMS treats patients in warm/cold zone
6. Treat — provide supportive care and agent-specific treatment per applicable protocols (10-2, 10-3, 10-5)
7. Transport — notify receiving facility of agent type; use appropriate PPE during transport

deterioration_flags: Any EMS provider with onset of symptoms consistent with chemical exposure (miosis, salivation, bronchospasm, seizures) — exit hot zone immediately, initiate self-treatment protocol
Mass casualty numbers exceeding local EMS capacity — activate MCI/SALT protocol (10-6)
Unknown agent without HazMat support — do NOT enter; stage and await resources
scope_notes: This protocol applies to initial recognition and scene safety for all CBRNE events. Specific treatment algorithms are in Protocols 10-2 (Chemical Exposure), 10-3 (Nerve Agent/Organophosphate), 10-4 (CHEMPACK/MEDDRUN), 10-5 (Cyanide), and 10-6 (Mass Casualty Incidents).
